period_summary: Citations cluster heavily in the 2nd–4th centuries, concentrated around Christological debates and the Passion narratives. The near-universal use of LXX text forms reveals the Septuagint's dominance as the authoritative Old Testament text in the early Greek-speaking church.

citations.cited_text: Θεέ μου, Θεέ μου, ἵνα τί ἐγκατέλιπές με; (My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?)
citations.text_form_note: Justin's citation uses 'ἵνα τί' (why) matching LXX Ps 21:2 exactly, whereas MT has 'לָמָה' (lamah). The verb 'ἐγκατέλιπές' aligns with LXX rather than any Hebraic alternative.
citations.theological_use: Justin employs this cry from the cross to demonstrate Christ's fulfillment of messianic prophecy, arguing that the Psalm predicts the crucifixion and vindicates Christian interpretation of Jewish Scripture against his Jewish dialogue partners.
citations.transmission_implication: Demonstrates early 2nd-century circulation of LXX Psalm text in Roman Christian communities with stable textual tradition.

citations.cited_text: Θεέ μου, Θεέ μου, πρόσχες μοι· ἵνα τί ἐγκατέλιπές με;
citations.text_form_note: Justin includes 'πρόσχες μοι' (give heed to me) matching LXX's fuller reading, showing familiarity with the complete Psalm text beyond the Gospel citation.
citations.theological_use: Used in anti-Jewish polemic to prove that Christ's suffering was prophetically foretold, with detailed exposition of the entire Psalm 22 as a Passion prophecy, linking abandonment to salvific suffering.
citations.transmission_implication: Attests a fuller LXX form circulating alongside the abbreviated Gospel citation, suggesting liturgical and exegetical use beyond Gospel texts.

citations.cited_text: Deus meus, Deus meus, ut quid dereliquisti me? (Latin: My God, my God, why have you abandoned me?)
citations.text_form_note: Irenaeus's Latin citation via Old Latin version reflects the LXX tradition's 'ἐγκατέλιπές' rendered as 'dereliquisti,' supporting Greek Vorlage dependency rather than direct Hebrew consultation.
citations.theological_use: Irenaeus uses the cry to demonstrate Christ's genuine human suffering and the reality of the Incarnation against Gnostic docetism, emphasizing that the divine Logos truly experienced abandonment in the flesh.
citations.transmission_implication: Shows LXX tradition mediated through Old Latin translations in Western Christianity by late 2nd century.

citations.cited_text: Deus meus, Deus meus, respice in me, ut quid me dereliquisti?
citations.text_form_note: Tertullian's Latin includes 'respice in me' (look upon me) corresponding to LXX 'πρόσχες μοι,' demonstrating use of fuller Psalm text beyond the truncated Gospel form.
citations.theological_use: Deployed against Marcion to prove continuity between Old Testament prophecy and Christ's Passion, arguing the Creator God's consistency across both testaments and refuting any disjunction between testaments.
citations.transmission_implication: Confirms Old Latin Psalm tradition in North Africa deriving from LXX, circulating independently of Gospel harmonies.

citations.cited_text: Θεέ μου Θεέ μου, ἵνα τί με ἐγκατέλιπες;
citations.text_form_note: Origen's citation precisely matches LXX Ps 21:2 with 'ἵνα τί' and 'ἐγκατέλιπες.' His Hexapla work confirms he knew both Hebrew and Greek forms but cites LXX in Greek exegetical contexts.
citations.theological_use: Origen interprets the cry allegorically and typologically: Christ assumes humanity's alienation from God, and his abandonment represents the redemptive bearing of human sin, not actual divine desertion.
citations.transmission_implication: Despite Hexaplaric access to multiple text forms, Origen's standard exegetical citation follows LXX, indicating its liturgical and theological primacy.

citations.cited_text: Ὁ Θεός, ὁ Θεός μου, πρόσχες μοι· ἵνα τί ἐγκατέλιπές με;
citations.text_form_note: Eusebius cites the fuller LXX form with 'πρόσχες μοι,' matching the Hexaplaric LXX column precisely, demonstrating continuity with Origen's critical text tradition.
citations.theological_use: Eusebius uses Psalm 22:1 as apologetic proof of prophecy fulfillment, embedding it within a comprehensive argument for Christianity's validation through predictive Old Testament witness to Christ's Passion.
citations.transmission_implication: Reflects stable LXX textual tradition in Caesarean scholarly circles post-Origen, with Hexaplaric scholarship reinforcing rather than displacing LXX primacy.

citations.cited_text: Ὁ Θεός μου, ὁ Θεός μου, ἵνα τί με ἐγκατέλιπες;
citations.text_form_note: Athanasius's citation employs standard LXX wording with 'ἵνα τί' and 'ἐγκατέλιπες,' consistent with Alexandrian liturgical and exegetical tradition uninfluenced by Hebraica veritas.
citations.theological_use: Athanasius interprets the cry Christologically to affirm Christ's full humanity—the Word truly experiences human dereliction—while maintaining ontological divine unity, crucial for pro-Nicene Christology against Arian subordinationism.
citations.transmission_implication: Demonstrates standardized LXX text in 4th-century Alexandrian orthodoxy, transmitted liturgically and used authoritatively in doctrinal formulation.

citations.cited_text: Θεέ μου, Θεέ μου, ἵνατί με ἐγκατέλιπες;
citations.text_form_note: Chrysostom's 'ἵνατί' (contracted form of 'ἵνα τί') represents Byzantine liturgical standardization of the LXX text, showing slight textual evolution toward vernacular Greek.
citations.theological_use: Chrysostom uses the cry pastorally, emphasizing Christ's solidarity with human suffering and modeling perseverance in faith during apparent divine absence, encouraging persecuted congregations through Christological example.
citations.transmission_implication: Reveals Byzantine liturgical influence on LXX text transmission by late 4th century, with minor morphological adaptations for homiletical use.

transmission_synthesis: The patristic citation history of Psalm 22:1 reveals absolute LXX dominance across all geographical regions and theological traditions through the 5th century. No Church Father cited a form closer to MT (Hebrew 'lamah' vs. LXX 'hina ti'), nor do mixed or independent readings appear, indicating remarkably stable textual transmission. Even text-critically sophisticated fathers like Origen and Eusebius, who had access to Hebrew and multiple Greek versions through the Hexapla, consistently cited the LXX form in their exegetical and theological works. The minor textual variations (e.g., inclusion/exclusion of 'prosches moi,' morphological variants like 'hinati') represent internal LXX tradition development rather than influence from non-LXX sources. This uniformity strongly supports the LXX's status as the exclusive biblical text of the early church for this passage, transmitted liturgically, exegetically, and dogmatically without substantive challenge from Hebrew or other Greek versions before Jerome's Vulgate.
transmission_synthesis_plain: Early Christian writers from the 2nd through 5th centuries unanimously used the Greek Septuagint version of Psalm 22:1, never citing the Hebrew text. This shows the Septuagint was the Bible of the early church, passed down through worship, teaching, and theological debate with remarkable stability across the entire Christian world.

notable_variants.reading: πρόσχες μοι (give heed to me / look upon me)
notable_variants.significance: This phrase, present in LXX Ps 21:2 but absent from the Gospel citations (Matt 27:46, Mark 15:34), distinguishes liturgical/exegetical Psalm use from Gospel harmony traditions. Fathers citing the fuller form demonstrate direct engagement with the Psalter independent of Gospel accounts, suggesting parallel transmission streams within the LXX tradition.

The morphological contraction from 'hina ti' to 'hinati' reflects Byzantine liturgical standardization and vernacular linguistic evolution in 4th-century Greek. This variant marks internal LXX development rather than textual corruption, important for dating stages of Byzantine recension.

bibcrit_assessment.title: Absolute LXX Monopoly: No Hebrew Text Influence
bibcrit_assessment.reasoning: The most striking finding is the complete absence of any MT-influenced readings across eight citations spanning three centuries and five regions. Even Origen, who compiled the Hexapla to compare Hebrew and Greek versions, exclusively cites the LXX form. This demonstrates that the Christian Old Testament existed as a closed LXX corpus by the 2nd century, insulated from ongoing Jewish Hebrew textual transmission. The uniformity has profound implications: Psalm 22:1's textual history in Christianity is exclusively a Greek LXX history until Jerome, meaning reconstructions of 'original' readings cannot rely on patristic evidence for pre-LXX Hebrew forms.
bibcrit_assessment.plain: Early church writers only knew Psalm 22:1 in Greek translation (the Septuagint), never in Hebrew. This tells us Christians used an exclusively Greek Old Testament for centuries, completely separate from the Hebrew Bible preserved by Jews.
